_about: Historiographical commentary — a scholarly-reflexive note for terms whose modern study is itself contested. Not 'what the word meant' (that is the History) but 'how scholars argue about it now': the problematics of 'shamanism', the new historiography of alchemy ('chymistry'), Kieckhefer's clerical necromantic underworld, Fanger on angel-magic-as-theurgy, the witch-cult debate, the Yates thesis. Drawn from the medieval/renaissance/alchemy databases, the PDF library, and Ted's own historiography discussions in C:\Dev\megabase. Rendered as a 'Historiographical note' aside on the Origin & History tab. See docs/templates/historiography_note.md.

shaman.commentary: 'Shaman' is a genuine Tungusic word (Evenki *šamán*), but *shamanism* — the universal category — is largely a Western construction. From Mircea Eliade onward, anthropology stretched a specific Siberian office to cover every trance-specialist on earth, and critics such as Alice Kehoe and Andrei Znamenski have shown how much that flattening owes to Romantic primitivism and a Cold-War appetite for a single 'archaic technique of ecstasy.' The word names something historically precise; the *-ism* names a scholarly habit worth distrusting.

sources.label: Alice B. Kehoe, Shamans and Religion
sources.abstract: Kehoe argues that the word "shaman," which comes from a specific Siberian people, has been stretched by Western writers to describe almost any spiritual healer worldwide, and that this loose, romanticized use of the term reveals more about Western fantasies of "primitive" cultures than about the real practices it claims to name.

sources.label: Andrei Znamenski, The Beauty of the Primitive
sources.abstract: This book traces how "shamanism," a word first used by explorers to describe Siberian religious specialists, was picked up by generations of Western writers, scholars, and spiritual seekers and reshaped into a symbol of nature-loving, anti-modern spirituality, often saying more about Western longings than about the Siberian traditions it was named after.

sources.label: Ronald Hutton, Shamans: Siberian Spirituality and the Western Imagination
sources.abstract: This book traces how Western writers and travelers, from medieval times through the Soviet era, imagined and often misunderstood the shamans (spiritual healers and mediums) of Siberia, and how those imagined images later shaped modern Western ideas about shamanism as a spiritual practice.

alchemy.commentary: Alchemy has been through a historiographical revolution. The Victorian and Jungian picture split a base 'exoteric' gold-making from a noble 'spiritual' alchemy of the soul — a division that, as Lawrence Principe and William Newman argued in 'Some Problems with the Historiography of Alchemy' (2001), the sources simply do not support. Recent scholarship prefers the period term *chymistry* for a single enterprise, at once laboratory practice and natural philosophy, integral to the Scientific Revolution rather than opposed to it. Tara Nummedal's work on craft and fraud pushed the field further toward the workshop and away from the psyche.

sources.label: Lawrence Principe & William Newman, 'Some Problems with the Historiography of Alchemy'
sources.abstract: A widely cited essay (a chapter in the book Secrets of Nature) arguing that many 20th-century scholars misread old alchemy as secret spiritual symbolism, when most alchemists were actually doing hands-on laboratory work aimed at real, physical transformations of matter.

sources.label: Tara Nummedal, 'Words and Works in the History of Alchemy' (Isis, 2011)
sources.abstract: Argues that historians should study alchemy not just as ideas on a page but as something people actually did with their hands, reading, writing, and running experiments all at once in settings ranging from royal courts to artisan workshops.

sources.label: Lawrence Principe, The Secrets of Alchemy
sources.abstract: A historian of chemistry who also recreates old experiments in his own laboratory, Principe explains what alchemists actually believed and did, arguing they were serious investigators of matter and nature rather than treasure-hunting con artists, and traces how their reputation shifted over time from respected practice to pseudoscience.

transmute.commentary: Transmutation looks like the mystical heart of alchemy, but the new historiography insists it was a serious material theory. William Newman's studies of the Latin Geber and of Newton's laboratory show transmutation resting on a coherent corpuscular matter-theory — the same intellectual world as early-modern *chymistry*. Read this way, the alchemist trying to turn lead to gold is not a dreamer but an experimentalist reasoning from premises that were, in their day, entirely rational.

sources.label: William R. Newman, Newton the Alchemist
sources.abstract: Shows that Isaac Newton, famous for physics and math, also spent decades secretly doing alchemy experiments, and uses Newton's own lab notes, plus modern recreations of his experiments, to figure out what he was actually trying to achieve.

sources.label: William R. Newman, on the Latin Geber (Summa Perfectionis)
sources.abstract: Investigates who really wrote the influential medieval Latin alchemy texts credited to "Geber" (supposedly the earlier Arabic alchemist Jabir ibn Hayyan), concluding the real author was most likely a European friar writing under that famous borrowed name.

sources.label: Lawrence Principe, The Secrets of Alchemy
sources.abstract: A historian of chemistry who also recreates old experiments in his own laboratory, Principe explains what alchemists actually believed and did, arguing they were serious investigators of matter and nature rather than treasure-hunting con artists, and traces how their reputation shifted over time from respected practice to pseudoscience.

necromancer.commentary: Behind the fantasy corpse-raiser lies a specific milieu that Richard Kieckhefer named the *clerical underworld*. The necromancy of the medieval manuals — his *Forbidden Rites* edits one, the Munich Handbook — was not peasant superstition but the illicit hobby of educated clergy: men with the Latin, the ritual training, and the leisure to conjure. In the sources, 'necromancy' is less a folk practice than an elite one, which is exactly why the authorities found it so alarming.

sources.label: Richard Kieckhefer, Forbidden Rites: A Necromancer's Manual of the Fifteenth Century
sources.abstract: A historian publishes and translates a real 15th-century handbook of "necromancy" found in a German library. It isn't about raising corpses -- it's a working magician's manual with prayers, lists of spirits to summon, and step-by-step rituals for consecrating a magic book, giving a rare inside look at how educated people actually practiced ritual magic in the late Middle Ages.

sources.label: Richard Kieckhefer, Magic in the Middle Ages
sources.abstract: A widely used introduction to what magic actually was in medieval Europe: who practiced it, what forms it took, and how it connected to religion, science, storytelling, and law. It also explains how fear of magic fed into the witch trials of later centuries.

sources.label: Frank Klaassen, The Transformations of Magic
sources.abstract: This book argues that medieval magic banned by the church came in two flavors: "image magic" (making charms and talismans), which scholars could half-justify with the science of the day, and "ritual magic" (rituals for summoning spirits), which could not. Klaassen traces how interest in the first kind faded after 1500 while the second kept going and helped shape early modern magic.

conjure.commentary: The conjuring texts belong to Kieckhefer's *clerical underworld* — a subculture of literate, often ordained men who copied and traded ritual manuals for binding spirits. Frank Klaassen distinguishes this learned, text-based 'ritual magic' from the image-and-astrology magic of the *Picatrix* tradition; both were the work of the educated, not the village. To conjure, historically, you needed a Latin schooling and a book — which is why the practice sat so uncomfortably close to the very clergy who condemned it.

sources.label: Richard Kieckhefer, Forbidden Rites
sources.abstract: A historian publishes and translates a real 15th-century handbook of "necromancy" found in a German library. It isn't about raising corpses -- it's a working magician's manual with prayers, lists of spirits to summon, and step-by-step rituals for consecrating a magic book, giving a rare inside look at how educated people actually practiced ritual magic in the late Middle Ages.

sources.label: Frank Klaassen, The Transformations of Magic
sources.abstract: This book argues that medieval magic banned by the church came in two flavors: "image magic" (making charms and talismans), which scholars could half-justify with the science of the day, and "ritual magic" (rituals for summoning spirits), which could not. Klaassen traces how interest in the first kind faded after 1500 while the second kept going and helped shape early modern magic.

theurgy.commentary: Claire Fanger's work reframed a whole class of medieval ritual magic as *theurgy* rather than demonic conjuring. The *Ars Notoria* and John of Morigny's *Liber Visionum* sought angelic knowledge through prayer and vision — operations their authors understood as devout, not diabolical. Reading them as theurgy (the Neoplatonic 'god-work' of Iamblichus) rather than *goetia* recovers how the practitioners saw themselves, and dissolves the tidy line the Church tried to draw between sanctioned religion and forbidden magic.

sources.label: Claire Fanger, Invoking Angels: Theurgic Ideas and Practices
sources.abstract: A collection of essays about medieval and early Renaissance rituals for contacting or working with angels (scholars call this "theurgy," meaning rituals aimed at divine or heavenly beings rather than ordinary spirits). The essays show how these angel-summoning traditions mixed Christian, Jewish, and Islamic ideas together.

sources.label: Claire Fanger (ed.), Conjuring Spirits
sources.abstract: A collection of scholarly essays examining actual medieval manuscripts used for ritual magic - instructions for summoning angels or demons, memory-boosting rites, and divination texts - to show how educated Christians in the Middle Ages practiced and argued about magic.

sources.label: Claire Fanger, Rewriting Magic (John of Morigny)
sources.abstract: A close study of a 14th-century French monk's own written account of creating a prayer ritual he believed could instantly grant knowledge — a project that got his book publicly condemned and burned as heresy in 1323. Fanger uses his firsthand story to explore how medieval people justified using something so close to forbidden magic.

goetia.commentary: The line between *theurgy* and *goetia* — high divine magic and low demonic magic — looks ancient and firm, but historians treat it as a boundary actively policed rather than simply found. What one age called pious angel-magic another prosecuted as demon-conjuring; the same operations could be *theurgia* or *goetia* depending on who held the pen. The category 'goetia' is, in no small part, a verdict.

sources.label: Claire Fanger, Invoking Angels
sources.abstract: A collection of essays about medieval and early Renaissance rituals for contacting or working with angels (scholars call this "theurgy," meaning rituals aimed at divine or heavenly beings rather than ordinary spirits). The essays show how these angel-summoning traditions mixed Christian, Jewish, and Islamic ideas together.

sources.label: David J. Collins, S.J. (ed.), The Sacred and the Sinister: Studies in Medieval Religion and Magic
sources.abstract: A collection of essays exploring how medieval people tried to draw a line between approved religious practice and forbidden magic — and how blurry and contested that line actually was in everyday life.

witch.commentary: No term in this study has a more contested historiography. Margaret Murray's thesis of a surviving pagan witch-cult (1921) was demolished by Norman Cohn's *Europe's Inner Demons*, which showed the sabbat to be a fantasy built by inquisitors from older libels. Keith Thomas set witchcraft in village society; Carlo Ginzburg's *The Night Battles* then recovered, beneath the trial records, the *benandanti* — a real folk-magic the courts could only hear as devil-worship. The 'witch,' the field now stresses, was largely a category made by prosecution.

sources.label: Norman Cohn, Europe's Inner Demons
sources.abstract: Traces where the medieval stereotype of witches flying off to secret orgies with the devil actually came from, and shows that key 'evidence' used to justify witch hunts was based on invented stories and outright forgeries rather than real observed practices.

sources.label: Carlo Ginzburg, The Night Battles
sources.abstract: Ginzburg reconstructs the beliefs of the benandanti ("good walkers"), a folk group in 16th-century northern Italy who said their spirits left their sleeping bodies at night to fight witches and protect the harvest, and traces how Church inquisitors gradually pressured them into confessing to being witches themselves.

sources.label: Keith Thomas, Religion and the Decline of Magic
sources.abstract: A landmark history of why belief in witchcraft, astrology, and protective charms was so common in 16th- and 17th-century England, and how the rise of Protestant religion and early science gradually pushed those beliefs to the margins of everyday life.

hermetic.commentary: To call something 'Hermetic' is to step into the most famous quarrel in the field. Frances Yates argued that a rediscovered Hermetic magic drove the Scientific Revolution — the 'Yates thesis' that launched the academic study of Western esotericism. It has been heavily qualified since: Wouter Hanegraaff and others show how much it owed to a Romantic grand narrative, and stress that 'Hermeticism' was never the single tradition Yates implied. The word is indispensable and treacherous in equal measure.

sources.label: Frances Yates, Giordano Bruno and the Hermetic Tradition
sources.abstract: A classic study arguing that the Renaissance philosopher Giordano Bruno, burned at the stake in 1600, was driven less by early modern science than by "Hermeticism" -- a set of ancient-seeming magical and mystical teachings he believed held the secret key to controlling nature.

sources.label: Wouter J. Hanegraaff, Esotericism and the Academy
sources.abstract: Explains why universities have historically dismissed astrology, alchemy, and magic as "not real knowledge," and argues that this rejection says as much about how scholars built their own identity as it does about the actual content of those traditions.

occult.commentary: 'The occult' as a unified field is younger than it looks. Wouter Hanegraaff has shown that 'Western esotericism' was assembled as a scholarly category only recently, gathering astrology, alchemy, magic, and Kabbalah into one object of study that earlier centuries did not treat as a single thing. The 19th-century coinage 'occultism' did comparable work. To speak of 'the occult sciences' is already to take a position in a debate about what belongs together.

sources.label: Wouter J. Hanegraaff, Esotericism and the Academy
sources.abstract: Explains why universities have historically dismissed astrology, alchemy, and magic as "not real knowledge," and argues that this rejection says as much about how scholars built their own identity as it does about the actual content of those traditions.

sources.label: Ronald Hutton, The Triumph of the Moon
sources.abstract: Traces how modern Wicca -- the pagan witchcraft religion that emerged in 20th-century England -- was actually assembled by blending older folklore, ceremonial magic, and Romantic ideas about nature, rather than being an unbroken survival of ancient paganism.

charm.commentary: No boundary in the field is argued harder than the one between a charm and a prayer. An older confessional view (echoed in medieval pastoral sources) treated the charm as *superstitio*, a debased or diabolical counterfeit of licit devotion. The turn, led by Keith Thomas and sharpened by Eamon Duffy, dissolved that line: the same formula could be prayer, medicine, or magic depending only on setting and authority. Don C. Skemer's *Binding Words* and the charm-studies of Lea Olsan and Catherine Rider now treat the charm as an ordinary, everyday kind of text, and Michael Bailey reframes 'superstition' itself as a classifying act, not a thing.

sources.label: Keith Thomas, Religion and the Decline of Magic
sources.abstract: A landmark history of why belief in witchcraft, astrology, and protective charms was so common in 16th- and 17th-century England, and how the rise of Protestant religion and early science gradually pushed those beliefs to the margins of everyday life.

sources.label: MedievalMagicDB, s.v. Charms (Olsan, Skemer, Rider)
Don C. Skemer, Binding Words
sources.abstract: Skemer studies small handwritten charms called textual amulets - strips of parchment inscribed with prayers or words of power that medieval people wore on their bodies or carried for protection - showing how these objects blurred the line between everyday religious devotion and magic.

curse.commentary: The curse-tablet is the classic battlefield for the 'magic versus religion' problem in antiquity. An older Frazerian view sorted the coercive *defixio* (magic) from the supplicant prayer (religion) as a difference of kind. H. S. Versnel undid the tidy split, showing 'prayers for justice' that beg the gods exactly as cursers command the dead, and John Gager's sourcebook demonstrates how thoroughly the *defixiones* borrow liturgical form. Recent work by Jessica Lamont and Stuart McKie pushes further, reading the tablets as ordinary social action rather than a deviant category — so that 'magic' becomes a label historians must justify, not assume.

sources.label: John G. Gager, Curse Tablets and Binding Spells from the Ancient World
sources.abstract: A translated collection of ancient Greek and Roman curse tablets -- small inscribed objects people buried or dedicated to gods to harm a rival, win a court case, or wreck a love affair -- showing how ordinary people used magic to handle everyday conflicts.

sources.label: Jessica L. Lamont, In Blood and Ashes
sources.abstract: Using newly published curse tablets alongside archaeological finds, Lamont shows that cursing rituals in ancient Greece were a common tool ordinary people -- tavern keepers, barbers, sailors -- used to settle everyday grudges and rivalries, not just a fringe practice of shady magicians.

sources.label: Stuart McKie, Living and Cursing in the Roman West
sources.abstract: Drawing on more than 600 ancient Roman curse tablets (small inscribed sheets of metal asking the gods to harm a rival or thief), this book looks at the everyday lives and social relationships of the ordinary people who wrote and buried them, treating cursing as a normal part of daily religious life rather than something strange or marginal.

bless.commentary: Where does a blessing end and a spell begin? An older Protestant-descended historiography read the Church's benedictions of salt, water, and fields as 'magic' thinly baptized. Keith Thomas gave that view its most influential form, describing a medieval Church that had developed 'a comprehensive range of formulae' barely distinguishable from charms. The turn was to question the dichotomy itself: Eamon Duffy recovered the sacramentals as coherent lay devotion, and S. J. Tambiah's 'The Magical Power of Words' reframed the whole religion/magic split as a Western analytic habit rather than a fact about the acts, so that blessing and enchantment stop being opposites.

sources.label: Keith Thomas, Religion and the Decline of Magic
sources.abstract: A landmark history of why belief in witchcraft, astrology, and protective charms was so common in 16th- and 17th-century England, and how the rise of Protestant religion and early science gradually pushed those beliefs to the margins of everyday life.

sources.label: S. J. Tambiah, 'The Magical Power of Words'
sources.abstract: Looking closely at a Sri Lankan exorcism ceremony, anthropologist Tambiah argues that spells and magic words "work" not because they're bad science but because of how ritual speech is performed - the repetition, rhythm, and formula make the words feel powerful, the same way a wedding vow or courtroom oath does real social work just by being spoken aloud.

sources.label: Eamon Duffy, The Stripping of the Altars
sources.abstract: Duffy argues that ordinary people in England deeply loved and actively took part in traditional Catholic religious life right up until the Protestant Reformation tore it down, pushing back against the older idea that the medieval church was corrupt and already unpopular before reformers arrived.

chant.commentary: Why should chanted words do anything at all? An older comparative view treated *incantatio* as the paradigm of 'magic' — mechanical, quasi-scientific coercion set against religion's petition. Renaissance magi themselves argued the mechanism, debating whether *incantatio* worked through the natural acoustic properties of sound as a vehicle of *spiritus*, through the objective force of divine names, or through demonic agency — the very line that divided *magia naturalis* from the illicit. S. J. Tambiah's 'The Magical Power of Words' turned the question inward, treating word-efficacy as performative ritual rather than failed physics, so the chant is now read as doing social work, not casting a mechanical spell.

sources.label: RenaissanceMagicDB, s.v. incantatio, fascinatio
S. J. Tambiah, 'The Magical Power of Words'
sources.abstract: Looking closely at a Sri Lankan exorcism ceremony, anthropologist Tambiah argues that spells and magic words "work" not because they're bad science but because of how ritual speech is performed - the repetition, rhythm, and formula make the words feel powerful, the same way a wedding vow or courtroom oath does real social work just by being spoken aloud.

sources.label: D. P. Walker, Spiritual and Demonic Magic from Ficino to Campanella
sources.abstract: Walker traces how Renaissance thinkers, from the philosopher Marsilio Ficino to the friar Tommaso Campanella, tried to draw a line between magic they considered natural and harmless and magic they worried actually called on demons, showing how these debates were tangled up with ideas about religion, music, and medicine.

possess.commentary: Modern scholarship treats possession less as a fact of the body than as a contested interpretation of one. An older clinical framing asked whether a given case was 'real' demonic possession, hysteria, or fraud. Nancy Caciola's *Discerning Spirits* dissolved that question by showing that the same trembling, fasting woman could be read as possessed by a demon or filled with the Holy Spirit — that *discretio spirituum*, the labor of telling which, was where the real history lay. Moshe Sluhovsky extends this, stressing how thin and negotiated the line between possession, mysticism, and exorcism actually was, a point the medieval hagiographic record bears out.

sources.label: Nancy Caciola, Discerning Spirits
sources.abstract: Caciola traces medieval methods for telling whether someone who seemed "possessed" was inhabited by a holy spirit, an evil one, or was just sick, showing that these judgments depended as much on the possessed person's gender and social standing as on religious doctrine.

sources.label: Moshe Sluhovsky, Believe Not Every Spirit
sources.abstract: This book explores why so many women in early modern Europe were accused of demonic possession, and how Catholic churchmen developed methods for judging whether a person's visions or trances came from God, from the devil, or were simply faked.

sources.label: David J. Collins, S.J. (ed.), The Sacred and the Sinister: Studies in Medieval Religion and Magic
sources.abstract: A collection of essays exploring how medieval people tried to draw a line between approved religious practice and forbidden magic — and how blurry and contested that line actually was in everyday life.

spell.commentary: 'Spell' carries the oldest assumption in the anthropology of magic: that words themselves are operative. James Frazer treated the spell as pseudo-science, a mistaken belief that saying makes it so. S. J. Tambiah's 'The Magical Power of Words' overturned that condescension, arguing that spells are performative acts whose force is ritual and social rather than a botched theory of cause. Don C. Skemer's *Binding Words* grounds the point materially, showing how written formulae worked as objects and practices, not failed physics — so the field now studies what a spell *does* in its setting rather than whether its speakers were being irrational.

sources.label: S. J. Tambiah, 'The Magical Power of Words'
sources.abstract: Looking closely at a Sri Lankan exorcism ceremony, anthropologist Tambiah argues that spells and magic words "work" not because they're bad science but because of how ritual speech is performed - the repetition, rhythm, and formula make the words feel powerful, the same way a wedding vow or courtroom oath does real social work just by being spoken aloud.

sources.label: Don C. Skemer, Binding Words
sources.abstract: Skemer studies small handwritten charms called textual amulets - strips of parchment inscribed with prayers or words of power that medieval people wore on their bodies or carried for protection - showing how these objects blurred the line between everyday religious devotion and magic.

sources.label: RenaissanceMagicDB, s.v. ligatura

exorcism.commentary: Older accounts treated exorcism as a stable Christian rite set cleanly against magic. The revisionist turn collapsed that line: Richard Kieckhefer notes that the *Twelve Conclusions* of 1395 could call the Church's own 'exorcisms and hallowings' the 'very practice of necromancy,' since exorcist and conjurer worked from the same repertoire of divine names and adjurations. Building on this, Moshe Sluhovsky (*Believe Not Every Spirit*) and Nancy Caciola (*Discerning Spirits*) reframe possession and its cure as a historically shaped contest over authority — who may command spirits, and how one tells the holy from the demonic.

sources.label: Richard Kieckhefer, Magic in the Middle Ages
sources.abstract: A widely used introduction to what magic actually was in medieval Europe: who practiced it, what forms it took, and how it connected to religion, science, storytelling, and law. It also explains how fear of magic fed into the witch trials of later centuries.

sources.label: Moshe Sluhovsky, Believe Not Every Spirit
sources.abstract: This book explores why so many women in early modern Europe were accused of demonic possession, and how Catholic churchmen developed methods for judging whether a person's visions or trances came from God, from the devil, or were simply faked.

sources.label: Nancy Caciola, Discerning Spirits
sources.abstract: Caciola traces medieval methods for telling whether someone who seemed "possessed" was inhabited by a holy spirit, an evil one, or was just sick, showing that these judgments depended as much on the possessed person's gender and social standing as on religious doctrine.

bewitch.commentary: It is tempting to read *bewitch* as naming a thing witches did. The historiography of witchcraft resists that: what the record preserves is overwhelmingly *accusation*. Richard Kieckhefer observes that the commonest charge was simply that some old woman had 'bewitched his cows or his children,' and Michael Bailey shows how the learned category of *maleficium* — harm attributed to occult means — gathered such complaints into a construction largely built by courts and confessors. The turn, associated with the microhistorical work of Ginzburg and others, is to study the witch as something *made* by prosecution rather than a practitioner recovered through it.

sources.label: Richard Kieckhefer, Magic in the Middle Ages
sources.abstract: A widely used introduction to what magic actually was in medieval Europe: who practiced it, what forms it took, and how it connected to religion, science, storytelling, and law. It also explains how fear of magic fed into the witch trials of later centuries.

sources.label: MedievalMagicDB, s.v. Maleficium
Michael D. Bailey, Magic and Superstition in Europe
sources.abstract: This single-volume overview traces how Europeans have understood, believed in, feared, and punished 'magic' and 'superstition,' from the ancient Near East and Greco-Roman world through the medieval and early modern witch trials, up to the spread of modern witchcraft movements like Wicca into the United States.

consecrate.commentary: The neat opposition of religion and magic long treated consecration as purely churchly. Keith Thomas unsettled it: the medieval Church deployed a vast repertoire of blessing and consecrating formulae — over holy bread, water, salt, oil — that laypeople valued for a power 'more than merely spiritual or symbolic,' so that the sacramentals shaded into the magical. Frank Klaassen presses from the other side, showing grimoires demanding ground and equipment be 'cleared and consecrated' in language lifted straight from liturgy. The field now stresses this shared ritual vocabulary rather than a policed line between sacrament and spell.

sources.label: Keith Thomas, Religion and the Decline of Magic
sources.abstract: A landmark history of why belief in witchcraft, astrology, and protective charms was so common in 16th- and 17th-century England, and how the rise of Protestant religion and early science gradually pushed those beliefs to the margins of everyday life.

sources.label: Frank Klaassen, The Transformations of Magic
sources.abstract: This book argues that medieval magic banned by the church came in two flavors: "image magic" (making charms and talismans), which scholars could half-justify with the science of the day, and "ritual magic" (rituals for summoning spirits), which could not. Klaassen traces how interest in the first kind faded after 1500 while the second kept going and helped shape early modern magic.

sources.label: RenaissanceMagicDB, s.v. exorcismus

invoke.commentary: The tidy pairing of *invoke* (calling a power in, upward, devoutly) against *evoke* (calling a spirit out, to compel it) is real in the grimoires — Solomonic handbooks speak of aerial spirits 'evoked and constrained' — but scholars caution against projecting that ceremonial precision back onto messier sources. Claire Fanger's work on the *Ars Notoria* and John of Morigny reframes much angelic invocation as *theurgy*, devout petition, dissolving the confident theurgy/goetia boundary the later systematizers drew. The distinction is a retrospective ordering of practices whose own authors often could not tell conjuration from prayer.

sources.label: Claire Fanger, Invoking Angels
sources.abstract: A collection of essays about medieval and early Renaissance rituals for contacting or working with angels (scholars call this "theurgy," meaning rituals aimed at divine or heavenly beings rather than ordinary spirits). The essays show how these angel-summoning traditions mixed Christian, Jewish, and Islamic ideas together.

sources.label: RenaissanceMagicDB, s.v. evocatio, theurgia
Frank Klaassen, The Transformations of Magic
sources.abstract: This book argues that medieval magic banned by the church came in two flavors: "image magic" (making charms and talismans), which scholars could half-justify with the science of the day, and "ritual magic" (rituals for summoning spirits), which could not. Klaassen traces how interest in the first kind faded after 1500 while the second kept going and helped shape early modern magic.

sublimate.commentary: The alchemical operations invite a special version of the alchemy debate. In the wake of Carl Jung and Mircea Eliade, *sublimatio*, *putrefactio*, and the rest were read as coded stages of the soul's transformation — an inner drama in chemical dress. Lawrence Principe and William Newman have insisted instead that these were real laboratory procedures: Zosimos used fire to separate spirits from bodies because he was actually subliming solids. Newman's study of Thomas Vaughan shows the period's own authors distinguishing operations on *physical substance* from any *unio mystica*, and Wouter Hanegraaff traces the purely 'spiritual' reading to a modern, and in his phrase 'degenerate,' construction.

sources.label: Lawrence Principe & William Newman, 'Some Problems with the Historiography of Alchemy'
sources.abstract: A widely cited essay (a chapter in the book Secrets of Nature) arguing that many 20th-century scholars misread old alchemy as secret spiritual symbolism, when most alchemists were actually doing hands-on laboratory work aimed at real, physical transformations of matter.

sources.label: William Newman, 'Thomas Vaughan as an Interpreter of Agrippa' (Ambix, 1982)
sources.abstract: Examines how the 17th-century alchemist Thomas Vaughan read and used the earlier writer Agrippa's ideas, arguing Vaughan understood alchemy as involving real physical processes rather than purely spiritual metaphor.

sources.label: Wouter J. Hanegraaff, Esotericism and the Academy
sources.abstract: Explains why universities have historically dismissed astrology, alchemy, and magic as "not real knowledge," and argues that this rejection says as much about how scholars built their own identity as it does about the actual content of those traditions.

mystic.commentary: 'Mysticism' looks timeless but is largely a modern coinage. Where older writers spoke of contemplation, union, or the *unio mystica* as specific practices, the nineteenth and twentieth centuries assembled a single universal category — 'mystical experience,' ineffable and cross-cultural — and read it back onto Eckhart, the Sufis, and the Kabbalists alike. Leigh Eric Schmidt has traced how liberal Protestant and Romantic writers manufactured that abstraction, and Michel de Certeau, in *The Mystic Fable*, showed 'the mystics' emerging as a distinct early-modern formation rather than a perennial one. The word names something real; the *-ism* names a scholarly habit.

sources.label: Leigh Eric Schmidt, 'The Making of Modern Mysticism' (JAAR, 2003)
sources.abstract: This journal article argues that today's popular idea of 'mysticism' — an intensely personal, direct spiritual experience separate from organized religion — is not an ancient concept but a fairly modern invention. Schmidt traces how it first took shape in English Enlightenment-era religious arguments and was then reshaped again by 19th-century American Transcendentalist thinkers.

sources.label: Michel de Certeau, The Mystic Fable
sources.abstract: This book examines Christian mysticism — the belief in a direct, personal encounter with God — as it appeared in Europe during the 1500s and 1600s. De Certeau treats mystical writing as a distinctive kind of language that emerged at a moment when older, shared religious certainties were starting to break down.

sources.label: Steven Katz, Mysticism and Philosophical Analysis
sources.abstract: This edited collection of essays argues that mystical experiences (intense, direct feelings of contact with the divine or ultimate reality) are shaped by the beliefs and culture of the person having them, meaning a Christian mystic and a Buddhist mystic do not actually have the identical experience described in different words.

grimoire.commentary: Older accounts treated the grimoire as a dead medieval relic — a curiosity of Solomonic seals surviving in a few manuscripts. Owen Davies's *Grimoires: A History* recast it as a living, mass-market, and global tradition: cheap print, colonial diffusion, and now the internet kept the spell-book continuously in circulation. Frank Klaassen sharpens the point from the other end, showing that the *printed* grimoire is largely a post-seventeenth-century formation — the *Grimoire du Pape Honorius* stitching medieval scraps into a new book. The field's turn is to read grimoires as *book history* rather than as occult oddities.

sources.label: Owen Davies, Grimoires: A History of Magic Books
sources.abstract: Davies traces the history of "grimoires," handbooks of magic spells, charms, and rituals, from the ancient Middle East to modern America. He shows that these books, often imagined as purely forbidden or sinister, actually helped spread literacy, religious ideas, and even early science over two thousand years.

sources.label: Frank Klaassen, The Transformations of Magic
sources.abstract: This book argues that medieval magic banned by the church came in two flavors: "image magic" (making charms and talismans), which scholars could half-justify with the science of the day, and "ritual magic" (rituals for summoning spirits), which could not. Klaassen traces how interest in the first kind faded after 1500 while the second kept going and helped shape early modern magic.

manuscript.commentary: Traditional philology hunted the single fixed *original* behind each text, treating variant copies as corruptions to be stripped away. The New Philology, and its application to magic by Frank Klaassen and Sophie Page, inverts that: for ritual texts the variation *is* the object. The *Ars Notoria* survived not as a stable book but as a fluid dossier of rewritten openings and recombined prayers, copied beside medical, astrological, and devotional matter. Klaassen's term for this is a *manuscript ecology* — a miscellany-culture that Benedek Láng and Kieckhefer's 'clerical underworld' fill out — where meaning lives in the mixing, not in a lost archetype.

sources.label: Frank Klaassen, The Transformations of Magic
sources.abstract: This book argues that medieval magic banned by the church came in two flavors: "image magic" (making charms and talismans), which scholars could half-justify with the science of the day, and "ritual magic" (rituals for summoning spirits), which could not. Klaassen traces how interest in the first kind faded after 1500 while the second kept going and helped shape early modern magic.

sources.label: Sophie Page, Magic in the Cloister
sources.abstract: A study of English monks who, despite their religious vows, collected and studied magic texts as part of their intellectual curiosity. It shows that for these monks, magic and monastic religious life weren't necessarily opposites — they could fit together.

sources.label: Benedek Láng, Unlocked Books
sources.abstract: Láng examines which handwritten books about magic - astrology, alchemy, and spirit-summoning texts - actually sat on the shelves of monasteries, royal courts, and universities in medieval Central Europe, showing that this kind of "learned magic" was a normal, if sometimes risky, part of intellectual life rather than something practiced only in secret.

bestiary.commentary: The romantic reading made the medieval bestiary proof of a 'symbolic mentality' — a world in which every beast was transparently a moral sign, the pelican simply *meaning* Christ. Recent work complicates the picture from two sides. Ron Baxter ties the genre's rise to a concrete institutional use, arguing that houses such as St. Augustine's, Canterbury promoted bestiaries as *sermon exempla* rather than as free-floating allegory. Sophie Page finds the same books shelved beside marvels and monstrous races — evidence of curiosity about nature's wonders, not only piety. The field now cautions against mistaking the allegory for the whole of medieval seeing.

sources.label: Frank Klaassen, The Transformations of Magic (on Ron Baxter)
sources.abstract: This book argues that medieval magic banned by the church came in two flavors: "image magic" (making charms and talismans), which scholars could half-justify with the science of the day, and "ritual magic" (rituals for summoning spirits), which could not. Klaassen traces how interest in the first kind faded after 1500 while the second kept going and helped shape early modern magic.

sources.label: Sophie Page, Magic in the Cloister
sources.abstract: A study of English monks who, despite their religious vows, collected and studied magic texts as part of their intellectual curiosity. It shows that for these monks, magic and monastic religious life weren't necessarily opposites — they could fit together.

herbal.commentary: An older progress-narrative sorted the herbal into a story of emerging *science*: superstitious plant-lore on one side, the birth of botany on the other, with the ritual husks discarded as the discipline matured. Historians now reject that clean split. Richard Kieckhefer stresses that the herbal never separated healing from wonder, growing 'a lush growth of lore about the wondrous powers of plants,' ritual harvesting instructions and all. Studies of early science make the same case: herbalism and bestiary lore were overlaid with religious and moral meaning that the later separation of magic from medicine retrospectively obscures rather than describes.

sources.label: Richard Kieckhefer, Magic in the Middle Ages
sources.abstract: A widely used introduction to what magic actually was in medieval Europe: who practiced it, what forms it took, and how it connected to religion, science, storytelling, and law. It also explains how fear of magic fed into the witch trials of later centuries.

sources.label: Religion, Magic, and the Origins of Science in Early Modern England
sources.abstract: John Henry collects his essays making the case that in England between roughly 1500 and 1700, religious belief and older ideas about magic were not obstacles to the rise of modern science. Instead, he argues, they often supplied the very motivations and mental tools early scientists used to investigate nature.

astrology.commentary: Astrology sits at the center of the field's biggest methodological quarrel: whether it belongs to the history of *science* or the history of *superstition*. Lynn Thorndike's monumental *History of Magic and Experimental Science* refused the tidy story of reason shedding astral nonsense, treating prediction and natural inquiry as intertwined. Later scholars — Nicolas Weill-Parot, David Pingree, and Paola Zambelli — sharpened the distinctions Thorndike blurred, separating astral image theory, mathematical practice, and judicial prognostication. The field now stresses that astrology was a rigorous technical discipline whose divorce from astronomy was late and contingent, not a failure waiting to be corrected.

sources.label: Lynn Thorndike, A History of Magic and Experimental Science
sources.abstract: This massive eight-volume history, published between 1923 and 1958, traces how magic and science developed together in Europe from the early Christian centuries through the 1600s. Thorndike's central argument is that early scientific thinking did not spring up in opposition to magic and the occult, but actually grew out of the same beliefs and practices.

sources.label: Nicolas Weill-Parot, on astrology and occult knowledge
sources.abstract: This detailed study (published as 'Les images astrologiques au Moyen Age et à la Renaissance') traces how medieval and Renaissance scholars tried to justify 'astrological images' — talismans believed to draw power down from the stars — as a form of natural science rather than forbidden magic. It shows how this argument shaped bigger debates about nature, technology, and the line between science and the occult.

sources.label: Paola Zambelli, Astrology and Magic from the Medieval Latin and Islamic World to Renaissance Europe
sources.abstract: A collection of the author's essays tracing how astrology (reading the stars to predict events) passed from Islamic and Latin medieval scholars into the Renaissance, and how astrologers actually worked as court advisors and public intellectuals, not just armchair theorists.

geomancy.commentary: The Greek name is a scholarly red herring, and the historiography turns on saying so. Despite *gē* ('earth'), the developed art was not Hellenic: it reached Latin Europe from the Arabic *ʿilm al-raml*, the 'science of sand,' and the field now reads geomancy as a case study in Arabic-Latin transmission. Stephen Skinner's revisionist history stresses its Islamic origin (the Greek magical papyri barely mention it), while Frank Klaassen shows the translated art circulating in learned miscellanies beside judicial astrology. The stress has shifted from etymology to transmission: geomancy is where a suspect 'clerical' art turns out to be imported science.

sources.label: MedievalMagicDB, s.v. Geomancy
Frank Klaassen, The Transformations of Magic
sources.abstract: This book argues that medieval magic banned by the church came in two flavors: "image magic" (making charms and talismans), which scholars could half-justify with the science of the day, and "ritual magic" (rituals for summoning spirits), which could not. Klaassen traces how interest in the first kind faded after 1500 while the second kept going and helped shape early modern magic.

sources.label: Stephen Skinner, Magical Techniques and Implements
sources.abstract: Skinner's doctoral thesis traces specific magic tools and step-by-step methods (like ritual instruments and spirit-summoning procedures) as they passed from ancient Greek-Egyptian magic texts, through Byzantine Greek manuscripts, into later European handbooks of magic — showing one continuous tradition rather than separate inventions.

clairvoyance.commentary: Unlike its ancient *-mancy* cousins, clairvoyance is a modern coinage, and that is precisely the historiographical point. The word was minted by nineteenth-century mesmerism and Spiritualism for the visions of entranced somnambulists, and the newer scholarship refuses to dismiss animal magnetism as mere quackery, reading the 'science of the séance' as serious contested science — the approach Alison Winter's *Mesmerized* exemplifies. Wouter Hanegraaff goes even further: 'to the extent that subjects believe in the veracity of their visions, they come to think of their experiences as instances of clairvoyance.' The field now treats the term less as a faculty than as an artifact of the movements that named it.

sources.label: Wouter J. Hanegraaff, Hermetic Spirituality and the Historical Imagination
sources.abstract: Looks at the ancient Egyptian writings attributed to the legendary teacher Hermes Trismegistus and argues they weren't just abstract philosophy — they describe real spiritual exercises meant to shift a person's state of mind so they could see past ordinary illusions to a deeper truth about reality.

sources.label: Alison Winter, Mesmerized: Powers of Mind in Victorian Britain
sources.abstract: Winter tells the story of "mesmerism," an early form of hypnosis said to work through an invisible magnetic fluid, showing that in Victorian Britain it wasn't a fringe curiosity but a widely debated practice that doctors, scientists, and ordinary people took seriously while arguing over where real science ended and quackery began.

sources.label: Hanegraaff, Forshaw & Pasi, Hermes Explains
sources.abstract: A collection of short, accessible essays by thirty scholars answering common questions about 'Western esotericism' -- the broad academic label for astrology, alchemy, magic, and other hidden-knowledge traditions -- aimed at clearing up popular misconceptions about the field.

hydromancy.commentary: Hydromancy is a lesson in distrusting neat taxonomies. Medieval and Renaissance writers reflexively listed it in an elemental quartet — geomancy, hydromancy, pyromancy, aeromancy — and scholars long took that scheme as descriptive. Stephen Skinner's revisionist reconstruction argues the symmetry is an artifact: 'it was only scholars who, copying Isidore of Seville, in his quest for a neat fourfold symmetrical classification, decided that hydromancy must have formed one of the four elemental forms of divination,' when in practice only geomancy really fitted. The MedievalMagicDB compilers make the complementary point — hydromancy survives as a category in condemnatory *lists* far more than as a documented practice. The field now reads such enumerations as classificatory imagination, not inventory.

sources.label: MedievalMagicDB, s.v. Hydromancy
Stephen Skinner, Magical Techniques and Implements
sources.abstract: Skinner's doctoral thesis traces specific magic tools and step-by-step methods (like ritual instruments and spirit-summoning procedures) as they passed from ancient Greek-Egyptian magic texts, through Byzantine Greek manuscripts, into later European handbooks of magic — showing one continuous tradition rather than separate inventions.

sources.label: MedievalMagicDB, s.v. Divination

druid.commentary: The druid is the classic case of an *invented* figure. The historical druids left no writings, and the little that survives — Caesar, Pliny, a scatter of Irish hagiography — is thin, hostile, and late; Kieckhefer cautions that the 'wizards' of Celtic legend are only 'distant reflections' of anything real. What most people picture is a double reconstruction: first the Renaissance ancient-theology tradition, which slotted the Druids beside Zoroaster and Hermes as gentile forerunners of true wisdom — a picture historians Frances Yates and Paola Zambelli both trace to that Renaissance move — and then the Romantic Celtic revival that the historian Ronald Hutton anatomizes in *Blood and Mistletoe*. The field now stresses that the druid is largely a screen for later longing.

sources.label: Ronald Hutton, Blood and Mistletoe: The History of the Druids in Britain
sources.abstract: Because almost nothing reliable actually survives about the ancient Druids (priests of pre-Roman Britain), this book instead tells the story of how later generations of British writers, antiquarians, and Romantic-era enthusiasts imagined and reinvented the Druids to fit their own changing ideas, across roughly two thousand years.

sources.label: Frances Yates, Giordano Bruno and the Hermetic Tradition
sources.abstract: A classic study arguing that the Renaissance philosopher Giordano Bruno, burned at the stake in 1600, was driven less by early modern science than by "Hermeticism" -- a set of ancient-seeming magical and mystical teachings he believed held the secret key to controlling nature.

sources.label: Richard Kieckhefer, 'The Specific Rationality of Medieval Magic'
sources.abstract: An influential essay arguing that medieval magic wasn't just ignorant superstition -- it followed its own internal logic, blending pieces of religion, early natural science, and folk tradition into systems that made coherent sense to the people who used them.

magus.commentary: For a generation the *magus* was a hero. Frances Yates cast the Renaissance Magus — Ficino, Pico, Agrippa, Bruno — as a bold new operator on nature whose confidence helped birth modern science. Critics have since dismantled the too-clean portrait: Håkan Håkansson calls the picture of Dee as a 'Hermetic magus' 'much too simplistic,' restoring his debt to Aristotle and medieval philosophy. Behind the honorific stands its shadow twin, Simon Magus, the New Testament's false wonder-worker; Kieckhefer and Edward Peters show how the Christian construction of 'the magician' fixed the word's ambivalence from the start. The field now reads the magus as a contested label, not a movement.

sources.label: Frances Yates, Giordano Bruno and the Hermetic Tradition
sources.abstract: A classic study arguing that the Renaissance philosopher Giordano Bruno, burned at the stake in 1600, was driven less by early modern science than by "Hermeticism" -- a set of ancient-seeming magical and mystical teachings he believed held the secret key to controlling nature.

sources.label: Håkan Håkansson, Seeing the Word: John Dee and Renaissance Occultism
sources.abstract: A doctoral thesis arguing that John Dee's strange mix of interests -- grammar, the Kabbalah, astronomy, alchemy, and math -- were not scattered hobbies but one connected project: recovering a lost perfect language Dee believed could unlock both nature's secrets and God's truth.

sources.label: Edward Peters, The Magician, the Witch, and the Law
sources.abstract: Peters traces how medieval Europe's view of magic changed over time: early on, magic was treated as a learnable (if suspect) skill, but law and church authorities gradually redefined it as heresy, turning practitioners once seen as skilled specialists into criminals who could be prosecuted as witches.

occultist.commentary: The job title is younger than the practice. 'Occultism,' and with it the 'occultist,' is a nineteenth-century coinage, born with Spiritualism and the revival of ceremonial magic, and the field now insists it not be read back onto earlier centuries. Wouter Hanegraaff and Marco Pasi have argued that 'occultism' — and the broader category 'Western esotericism' — are recently constructed scholarly objects: Agrippa spoke of *philosophia occulta*, not of being an occultist. The turn is to treat the noun as a historical artifact of modernity's own polemics (the 'occult' as the rejected, disenchanted remainder) rather than a timeless kind of person.

sources.label: Wouter Hanegraaff, Peter Forshaw & Marco Pasi (eds.), Hermes Explains
sources.abstract: This is a collection of short, plain-language answers by leading scholars to thirty basic questions about Western esotericism (a broad label for historical traditions like alchemy, astrology, and magic that mainstream religion and science pushed to the margins), aimed at readers with no prior background in the subject.

sources.label: Wouter Hanegraaff, Esotericism and the Academy
sources.abstract: Explains why universities have historically dismissed astrology, alchemy, and magic as "not real knowledge," and argues that this rejection says as much about how scholars built their own identity as it does about the actual content of those traditions.

sources.label: Cornelius Agrippa, De Occulta Philosophia
sources.abstract: Written in the early 1500s, this is Renaissance scholar Cornelius Agrippa's sweeping handbook of magic, describing three connected levels of hidden power -- in nature, in the stars, and in the divine -- and explaining how a learned magician could try to tap into each one; this edition is the standard modern scholarly edition of the original Latin text.

exorcist.commentary: Exorcism once looked like a stable Christian rite standing safely apart from magic. Recent scholarship makes it a *boundary* practice instead. MedievalMagicDB classes it a 'Hybrid': the exorcist and the conjurer often worked from the same page of adjurations and divine names, and Kieckhefer notes the exorcist's stance was 'at once coercive and petitionary,' indistinguishable in form from the necromancer's. Stuart Clark and historians of possession show the phenomenon as historically shaped — a contested performance policed by institutions, not a timeless clinical fact — so that what counted as demonic, and who could licitly expel it, was itself the argument.

sources.label: Richard Kieckhefer, Magic in the Middle Ages
sources.abstract: A widely used introduction to what magic actually was in medieval Europe: who practiced it, what forms it took, and how it connected to religion, science, storytelling, and law. It also explains how fear of magic fed into the witch trials of later centuries.

sources.label: Stuart Clark, Thinking with Demons
sources.abstract: Looks at how educated Europeans between the 1400s and 1700s wrote about witches and demons, and argues these ideas were not fringe nonsense but fit logically with how those same thinkers understood nature, religion, history, and politics at the time.

sources.label: MedievalMagicDB, s.v. Exorcism (Hybrid category)

conjurer.commentary: The stage 'conjurer' obscures a sharper historical figure. Richard Kieckhefer located ritual conjuration in what he called the *clerical underworld* — literate, often ordained men who alone had the Latin and liturgical training to command spirits. In his reading the conjurer's stance mirrors the exorcist's, 'at once coercive and petitionary,' following 'the spirit — and often the letter — of orthodox exorcism.' Claire Fanger and Frank Klaassen extended this into a study of illicit *learned* magic as it spread through hand-copied manuscripts. The field now stresses that conjuring was not folk deviance but a shadow of clerical literacy itself.

sources.label: Richard Kieckhefer, Magic in the Middle Ages
sources.abstract: A widely used introduction to what magic actually was in medieval Europe: who practiced it, what forms it took, and how it connected to religion, science, storytelling, and law. It also explains how fear of magic fed into the witch trials of later centuries.

sources.label: Claire Fanger (ed.), Conjuring Spirits: Texts and Traditions of Medieval Ritual Magic
sources.abstract: A collection of scholarly essays examining actual medieval manuscripts used for ritual magic - instructions for summoning angels or demons, memory-boosting rites, and divination texts - to show how educated Christians in the Middle Ages practiced and argued about magic.

sources.label: Frank Klaassen, The Transformations of Magic
sources.abstract: This book argues that medieval magic banned by the church came in two flavors: "image magic" (making charms and talismans), which scholars could half-justify with the science of the day, and "ritual magic" (rituals for summoning spirits), which could not. Klaassen traces how interest in the first kind faded after 1500 while the second kept going and helped shape early modern magic.

hierophant.commentary: The Eleusinian hierophant has been repeatedly borrowed to license a modern idea of the sage. Already in late antiquity, as György Szönyi shows, Neoplatonism 'turned the philosopher into a hierophant,' recasting the priest who displayed sacred objects as one who unveils divine reality — a move Renaissance occultism inherited. The category was stretched further in the twentieth century: Mircea Eliade coined *hierophany* ('manifestation of the sacred') as a universal analytic that Renaissance figures never used. Scholars now treat 'the Mysteries' and their officiants warily, alert to how much later philosophy and modern religious studies have read *into* a cult about which the initiates kept silent.

sources.label: György Szönyi, John Dee's Occultism
sources.abstract: Argues that John Dee's wide-ranging interests in science, mathematics, and angel-summoning were all held together by one goal: his belief that humans could spiritually elevate themselves toward a god-like state through hidden knowledge and symbols.

sources.label: Mircea Eliade, Patterns in Comparative Religion (on hierophany)
sources.abstract: This wide-ranging 1958 survey compares religious symbols and practices from many different cultures and time periods — things like sky gods, sacred stones, the moon, and fertility rites — to argue that similar patterns of 'the sacred' (what people treat as set apart and holy) show up again and again across human history.

sources.label: RenaissanceMagicDB, s.v. hierophany

fetish.commentary: No word better exposes how a category can be manufactured at a border. *Fetish* was not an African term but a coinage of Portuguese traders on the West African coast, systematized into a theory of primitive religion by Charles de Brosses and then annexed by the metropolitan disciplines — Marx for the *commodity*, Freud for desire. William Pietz's *The Problem of the Fetish* is the decisive intervention: the concept, he argues, was born in the intercultural trade encounter, not in Africa, and the scholarly habit of naming other people's objects 'fetishes' says more about European anxieties than about the objects.

sources.label: William Pietz, 'The Problem of the Fetish'
sources.abstract: This is the first installment of a three-part study tracing how the idea of a 'fetish' (an object believed to hold magical power) was invented by European traders describing West African religious objects in the 1500s-1600s, and how that idea later got applied, often unfairly, to describe other cultures' beliefs.

sources.label: Hanegraaff, Forshaw & Pasi, Hermes Explains
sources.abstract: A collection of short, accessible essays by thirty scholars answering common questions about 'Western esotericism' -- the broad academic label for astrology, alchemy, magic, and other hidden-knowledge traditions -- aimed at clearing up popular misconceptions about the field.

sources.label: Brian Copenhaver, Magic and the Dignity of Man
sources.abstract: Copenhaver challenges the popular idea that Renaissance philosopher Pico della Mirandola's famous speech was a humanist manifesto about human free will and dignity; he argues the text was really about magic and Jewish mysticism (Kabbalah), and that its modern reputation as being about "the dignity of man" is a five-century-long case of mistaken memory.

rune.commentary: The gap here is between a thin medieval record and a lavish modern system. Kieckhefer notes the sources give us Woden's mastery of the runic alphabet and healing charms, and the *Libri runarum* fused Scandinavian runes with hermetic talismanic magic — but nothing like the comprehensive 'runic magic' of later occultism. That system is largely a nineteenth- and twentieth-century construction: the Germanic esotericism of Guido von List, the SS appropriation of runic sigils, and neo-pagan reconstruction. Ronald Hutton and others caution against reading these elaborate schemes back onto the medieval evidence; the modern *-ism* is the invention, not the letters.

sources.label: Richard Kieckhefer, Magic in the Middle Ages
sources.abstract: A widely used introduction to what magic actually was in medieval Europe: who practiced it, what forms it took, and how it connected to religion, science, storytelling, and law. It also explains how fear of magic fed into the witch trials of later centuries.

sources.label: Page & Rider, The Routledge History of Medieval Magic
sources.abstract: A large reference collection that rounds up current scholarship on magic in medieval Europe (roughly 1100 to 1500), covering the texts people used, how the church and universities reacted, and how magic touched gender roles, art, medicine, and the early witch trials.

sources.label: Ronald Hutton, on modern paganism and the runic revival
sources.abstract: This refers to historian Ronald Hutton's broader research into how modern Pagan and occult movements, starting in the 1800s, revived old Germanic and Norse rune alphabets (originally just letters used for writing) as tools for magic, divination, and national identity. No single, specific title matching this exact description could be verified against a real public webpage, so no link is given here.

talisman.commentary: The talisman sits on the great fault line of Renaissance magic: was drawing planetary power into an image *natural* or *demonic*? D. P. Walker's *Spiritual and Demonic Magic* framed the problem around Ficino's *De vita*, which tried to keep astral talismans within *magia naturalis* — a capture of *influxus* through material correspondence — while nervously citing the *Picatrix*; Brian Copenhaver has since shown how unstable that line was. Medievalists add a second worry: Nicolas Weill-Parot and Jean-Patrice Boudet argue that 'talisman' is too smooth a modern label, flattening the distinct theories of image-efficacy the sources actually kept apart.

sources.label: D. P. Walker, Spiritual and Demonic Magic from Ficino to Campanella
sources.abstract: Walker traces how Renaissance thinkers, from the philosopher Marsilio Ficino to the friar Tommaso Campanella, tried to draw a line between magic they considered natural and harmless and magic they worried actually called on demons, showing how these debates were tangled up with ideas about religion, music, and medicine.

sources.label: MedievalMagicDB, s.v. Talismans (Weill-Parot, Boudet)
Attrell & Porreca, Picatrix
sources.abstract: The first full English translation of a medieval how-to manual, originally written in Arabic and later translated into Latin, that teaches readers how to build magical charms, mix potions, and use astrology to draw on the supposed powers of the planets. It became one of the most influential sourcebooks for later European magic.

grail.commentary: The Grail's sanctity was retrofitted, and modern scholarship has learned to distrust its own myth-making. In Chrétien's *Perceval* the *graal* is an unexplained serving-dish; only when clerical authors reworked the romance, Kieckhefer observes, did the religious matter thicken into the Last Supper's chalice. The twentieth century then re-mythologized it: Jessie Weston's *From Ritual to Romance* traced the Grail to a pagan fertility cult — an argument that shaped Eliot and Frazer's afterlife but that Arthurian scholars have largely rejected as speculative. The field now stresses the Grail as a literary-Christian construction, not the survival of an archaic rite.

sources.label: Richard Kieckhefer, Magic in the Middle Ages
sources.abstract: A widely used introduction to what magic actually was in medieval Europe: who practiced it, what forms it took, and how it connected to religion, science, storytelling, and law. It also explains how fear of magic fed into the witch trials of later centuries.

sources.label: Jessie Weston, From Ritual to Romance
sources.abstract: This early-1900s study argues that the medieval legends about the search for the Holy Grail (the sacred cup in King Arthur stories) did not start as purely Christian tales, but grew out of much older fertility rituals — ceremonies meant to keep crops, animals, and land healthy and productive. The book later became famous for directly inspiring T. S. Eliot's poem 'The Waste Land.'

crystal-ball.commentary: The crystal ball's historiography turns on John Dee, and on how badly the Victorians misjudged him. Dee's diaries record the 'shew-stone,' the crystal globes, and the angel-conversations relayed through his scryer Edward Kelley — long dismissed as the credulous folly of a great mind gone to seed. Deborah Harkness's *John Dee's Conversations with Angels* overturned that verdict, reading the scrying sessions as a coherent, learned program of *cabala*, alchemy, and natural philosophy rather than crankery; Glyn Parry's *The Arch Conjuror of England* sets them in their political world. The field now treats Elizabethan scrying as serious intellectual history.

sources.label: Deborah Harkness, John Dee's Conversations with Angels
sources.abstract: Explores why the Elizabethan scholar John Dee spent his later years trying to talk to angels through a psychic medium, arguing these sessions grew naturally out of his earlier scientific and religious interests rather than being a sign he had lost his mind.

sources.label: Glyn Parry, The Arch Conjuror of England
sources.abstract: A biography arguing that John Dee was not just an eccentric magician on the sidelines of history, but a real political operator whose magic and angel-conversations were tangled up with Elizabethan court intrigue and Europe's religious conflicts.

sources.label: György Szönyi, John Dee's Occultism
sources.abstract: Argues that John Dee's wide-ranging interests in science, mathematics, and angel-summoning were all held together by one goal: his belief that humans could spiritually elevate themselves toward a god-like state through hidden knowledge and symbols.

amulet.commentary: The amulet is where the old 'magic versus religion' partition breaks down. Keith Thomas showed that the medieval Church itself encouraged 'ecclesiastical talismans and amulets,' making the line between sanctioned sacramental and illicit charm 'impossibly fine.' Recent scholarship presses further: Don Skemer's *Binding Words* reads written amulets as devotional manuscript culture, 'books in miniature,' and MedievalMagicDB treats 'amulet' frankly as an *analyst term* — a modern grouping imposed on objects medieval actors variously called blessings, remedies, or *phylacteria*. The debate is less about what amulets did than about whether our category smooths over distinctions the sources kept rough.

sources.label: Keith Thomas, Religion and the Decline of Magic
sources.abstract: A landmark history of why belief in witchcraft, astrology, and protective charms was so common in 16th- and 17th-century England, and how the rise of Protestant religion and early science gradually pushed those beliefs to the margins of everyday life.

sources.label: MedievalMagicDB, s.v. Amulets (Skemer, Olsan)

cauldron.commentary: The cauldron looks timeless — Medea and Circe already brew in one — but the *witch's* cauldron is a datable invention. Charles Zika's *The Appearance of Witchcraft* argues that the stock image of women clustered around a steaming pot was assembled in sixteenth-century print and visual culture, not inherited from folk belief: engravings by Baldung and de Gheyn built a new iconography of sexual and demonic threat, of which the cauldron became a defining prop. The field now reads the object as evidence for how the witch was *pictured* into being, a construction later fixed by Shakespeare's weird sisters.

sources.label: Charles Zika, The Appearance of Witchcraft
sources.abstract: Zika studies printed woodcuts and illustrated pamphlets from 16th-century Europe to show how artists invented and spread the now-familiar image of the witch (flying, naked, cooking over a cauldron), arguing these pictures shaped public fear of witchcraft as much as trial records or sermons did.

sources.label: Charles Zika, 'Cannibalism and Witchcraft in Early Modern Europe'
sources.abstract: Zika looks at prints and paintings from early modern Europe that show witches supposedly cooking and eating babies, arguing that these shocking images reflected deep social anxieties about community breakdown and moral corruption rather than any real evidence that such cannibalism happened.

haruspex.commentary: The *haruspex* practiced the Etruscan *disciplina*, and reconstructing it is a notorious problem. The Etruscans' own ritual books are lost, and almost everything we know arrives through Roman and later hands that were often hostile, antiquarian, or both. Jean MacIntosh Turfa has worked to recover the system from artifacts — the bronze Piacenza Liver, the Brontoscopic Calendar — while cautioning that Roman writers reshaped Etruscan religion to their own ends. The field now stresses how much of 'Etruscan divination' is a Roman and modern reconstruction of a tradition that left few words of its own.

sources.label: Jean MacIntosh Turfa, Divining the Etruscan World
sources.abstract: This book gives the first complete English translation of the 'Brontoscopic Calendar,' an ancient text that told the Etruscans (a people who lived in what is now Italy before the Romans) what different days' thunder was supposed to mean for the future. It explains how this calendar fit into Etruscan religious life and traces its roots back to even older Mesopotamian methods of predicting events from natural signs.

sources.label: Georg Luck, Arcana Mundi
sources.abstract: This book collects and translates ancient Greek and Roman writings about magic, witches, and the occult, giving readers direct access to primary sources that show how ancient people actually talked about spells, curses, and supernatural power.

sibyl.commentary: The Sibyl is inseparable from the *Sibylline Oracles*, and those are a scholarly minefield. The surviving collection is not pagan prophecy but a layered Jewish-Hellenistic and then Christian pseudepigraphon — verse forged, expanded, and re-forged over centuries to put monotheist and Christian prediction into a venerable pagan mouth. John J. Collins and H. W. Parke have untangled these strata, and the field now reads the Sibyl less as an oracle than as a much-appropriated literary authority, claimed by each tradition that needed a pre-Christian witness to its own truth.

sources.label: John J. Collins, on the Sibylline Oracles (Old Testament Pseudepigrapha)
sources.abstract: Based on his Harvard doctoral research, John J. Collins's 'The Sibylline Oracles of Egyptian Judaism' studies a set of ancient prophetic poems credited to the 'Sibyl,' a legendary Greek prophetess. Collins shows these particular oracles were actually composed by Jewish writers living in Egypt, who borrowed a popular pagan literary style to spread their own religious and political messages.

sources.label: H. W. Parke, Sibyls and Sibylline Prophecy in Classical Antiquity
sources.abstract: Parke traces the legendary Sibyls -- prophetic women in the ancient Mediterranean believed to deliver cryptic verse prophecies -- from their earliest appearances in Greek sources through their adoption by the Romans and, later, early Christians who reinterpreted their sayings as predictions of Christ.

oneiromancy.commentary: Dream-divination's modern historiography runs through Artemidorus' *Oneirocritica* and the twentieth century's fascination with it. Freud cited him; Michel Foucault made the *Oneirocritica* the centerpiece of *The Care of the Self*, reading it for an ethics of the dreaming subject rather than a key to the future; S. R. F. Price ('The Future of Dreams') recovered it instead as a social practice woven into ancient status and exchange. The debate is less about what dreams 'mean' than about how a second-century dream-book became a mirror in which each era reads its own theory of the self.

sources.label: S. R. F. Price, 'The Future of Dreams: From Freud to Artemidorus'
sources.abstract: This article compares how the ancient Greek dream-interpretation manual by Artemidorus explained dreams to how Sigmund Freud later explained them, arguing that ancient dream interpreters were doing something different from modern psychology rather than a cruder version of the same thing.

sources.label: Michel Foucault, The Care of the Self
sources.abstract: The third volume of Foucault's History of Sexuality looks at how Greek and Roman writers in the first two centuries CE thought about pleasure, self-discipline, and bodily health. Foucault argues that during this period attitudes shifted toward more caution and anxiety about sex, compared with earlier classical Greek views.

esoteric.commentary: 'Esoteric' now names an academic discipline, and how to define it is itself the debate. Antoine Faivre proposed four defining traits — correspondences, living nature, imagination, transmutation — that gave 'Western esotericism' a workable shape. Wouter Hanegraaff then pressed an *empirical*, historicist turn, treating esotericism as the 'rejected knowledge' of Western culture rather than a perennial essence, and faulting the older 'religionist' Eranos school (Eliade, Corbin) for smuggling belief into scholarship. To call something esoteric is to enter a live methodological quarrel about whether the category describes a real current or constructs one.

sources.label: Wouter J. Hanegraaff, Esotericism and the Academy
sources.abstract: Explains why universities have historically dismissed astrology, alchemy, and magic as "not real knowledge," and argues that this rejection says as much about how scholars built their own identity as it does about the actual content of those traditions.

sources.label: Antoine Faivre, Access to Western Esotericism
sources.abstract: Faivre, one of the founders of the academic study of Western esotericism (hidden or symbolic spiritual traditions like alchemy and astrology), gives a beginner-friendly overview tracing these currents of thought from ancient times through the Renaissance up to the present day.

codex.commentary: Why did the bound *codex* replace the scroll? It is one of book history's great puzzles. Colin Roberts and T. C. Skeat, in *The Birth of the Codex*, showed the shift was led disproportionately by early Christians, who adopted the codex for scripture far ahead of the wider book trade — but the reason stays disputed: sheer convenience, a deliberate break from Jewish scroll-culture, or the prestige of one early authoritative codex. The field treats the codex not as a natural improvement that simply won, but as a contingent choice whose cause is still argued.

sources.label: Colin H. Roberts & T. C. Skeat, The Birth of the Codex
sources.abstract: This short study explains how the modern book format -- separate pages bound together, called a codex -- replaced the ancient scroll, and argues that early Christians were the ones who most enthusiastically adopted this new, more practical way of storing text.

quintessence.commentary: The quintessence is a test case for the new historiography of alchemy. An older reading treated John of Rupescissa's distilled 'fifth essence' as mystical fancy; Leah DeVun's *Prophecy, Alchemy, and the End of Time* recovers something stranger and more concrete — a real distillation science yoked to Franciscan apocalypticism, a heavenly incorruptible meant to fortify the body for the Last Days. The point is the one Principe and Newman press generally: what looks like spiritual metaphor was often laboratory medicine *with* a theological purpose, not instead of one.

sources.label: Leah DeVun, Prophecy, Alchemy, and the End of Time: John of Rupescissa
sources.abstract: Tells the story of a 14th-century friar, John of Rupescissa, who believed the end of the world was near and argued that alchemy could produce medicines and materials to help people survive the coming disasters.

sources.label: Lawrence Principe, The Secrets of Alchemy
sources.abstract: A historian of chemistry who also recreates old experiments in his own laboratory, Principe explains what alchemists actually believed and did, arguing they were serious investigators of matter and nature rather than treasure-hunting con artists, and traces how their reputation shifted over time from respected practice to pseudoscience.

potion.commentary: The love-potion opens onto one of the liveliest areas of ancient-magic scholarship. Christopher Faraone's *Ancient Greek Love Magic* argued that erotic magic split by gender and aim: violent *agōgē* spells to seize and compel, used mostly by men, against gentler *philia* potions to keep affection, associated with women — and that the sources' panic over women poisoning men (*pharmaka* meaning both potion and poison) encodes real anxieties about female agency. The debate turns the love-potion from a quaint charm into evidence about gender, power, and who was permitted to desire.

sources.label: Christopher A. Faraone, Ancient Greek Love Magic
sources.abstract: Faraone studies real spells, potions, and charms that ancient Greeks used to make someone fall in love, stay faithful, or come back to them, and shows that these love-magic recipes worked very differently depending on whether a man or a woman was doing the casting.

sources.label: Hans Dieter Betz, The Greek Magical Papyri in Translation
sources.abstract: A translated collection of real spells, charms, and magical recipes written on papyrus in Greco-Roman Egypt roughly 1,600 to 2,200 years ago, giving readers direct access to what ordinary magical practice actually looked like rather than what critics said about it.

poppet.commentary: The witch's doll was long dismissed as folklore, but archaeology made it a serious object. The pierced figurines described in the Greek Magical Papyri, and the lead-and-wax 'poppets' buried with binding tablets at the Fountain of Anna Perenna in Rome, show image-magic as a widespread ancient practice rather than a later fantasy. Christopher Faraone and Daniel Ogden read these figures as evidence for how binding magic actually worked — deforming an effigy to reach its target at a distance. The turn is from treating the poppet as superstition to treating it as a technology with a real material record.

sources.label: Daniel Ogden, Witchcraft and Magic in Europe: Greece and Rome
sources.abstract: This collection gathers essays by several scholars on magic in the ancient Greek and Roman world, including Daniel Ogden's chapter on curse tablets and "voodoo dolls" used to bind or harm enemies, alongside chapters on witches in literature and how early Christians later recast old pagan magic as demonic.

sources.label: Christopher A. Faraone, on binding figurines (katadesmoi)
sources.abstract: This 1991 journal article examines small clay or wax dolls that ancient Greeks bound with cords and buried in the ground to trap a dangerous spirit or curse a rival, arguing that these "voodoo dolls" were used mainly to hold a threat in place rather than to destroy it.
